coredata.dc:title: Thioether-bridged carbon dots-dopamine quinone conjugate probe for glutathione sensing and discrimination between glioblastoma and normal cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Ningcheng
Xiao, Yi
Jin, Yang
Zhang, Liying
Chen, Panpan
Du, Shuhu
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and specific recognition and imaging of glioblastoma (GBM) cells are crucial for early diagnosis in GBM. Herein, a novel thioether-bridged fluorescence carbon dots (CDs@S for short)-dopamine quinone (DAQ) conjugate (termed as CDs@S-DAQ) probe with red emission was fabricated for sensitive sensing of glutathione (GSH) and selective fluorescence imaging of GBM cells. Due to the strong photo-induced electron transfer (PET) effect between DAQ (the acceptor) and CDs@S (the donor), the red fluorescence of CDs@S is significantly quenched. In the presence of GSH, on the one hand, GSH reduces the quinone structure to a hydroquinone structure via Michael addition reaction, which eliminates the PET effect between DAQ and CDs@S, resulting in the fluorescence restoring of CDs@S; on the other hand, it simultaneously induces the cleavage of the thioether bond linking DAQ and CDs, causing DAQ to detach from the CDs surface and reducing the PET effect, which leads to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. It has been demonstrated that the response of CDs@S-DAQ probe to GSH has a good linear relationship in the range from 0.5 to 10 mM, with an R2 value of 0.9978. Meanwhile, the as-fabricated CDs@S-DAQ probe has excellent stability, superior optical properties and low cytotoxicity. Based on these findings, the probe can be applied in live-cell fluorescence imaging, enabling discrimination between GBM cells and normal tissue cells according to their distinct GSH expression levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red-emitting carbon dots
Thioether bond
Surface functionalization
GSH-responsive fluorescent probe
Glioblastoma cells imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs@S-DA and CDs@S-DAQ conjugates
Measurement of GSH in aqueous solution
Cell cytotoxicity assay of CDs@S-DAQ probe
Preparation and characterizations of CDs@S-DAQ probe
Sensitivity and selectivity for GSH detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22274080 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province ( 2025ZNSFSC1557 ) and the Science and Technology Strategic Cooperation Programs of Luzhou Municipal People\u2019s Government and Southwest Medical University ( 2024LZXNYDJ046 ).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274080), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2025ZNSFSC1557) and the Science and Technology Strategic Cooperation Programs of Luzhou Municipal People's Government and Southwest Medical University (2024LZXNYDJ046).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HRTEM images of CDs@S-DAQ. (C) TEM histogram and Gauss fitting of particle size distribution for CDs@S-DAQ. (D) FT-IR spectra of CDs (ⅰ), CDs@S (ⅱ), CDs@S-DA (ⅲ) and CDs@S-DAQ (ⅳ). XPS survey spectrum of (E) CDs, (F) CDs@S and (G) CDs@S-DAQ. (H) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs@S and CDs@S-DAQ.

(A) Evolution of fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs@S over irradiation time, respectively. (B) Evolution of fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs@S over pH, respectively. (C) Evolution of fluorescence intensity of CDs with different pH over incubation time, respectively. (D) Evolution of fluorescence intensity of CDs@S with different pH over incubation time, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) evolution of fluorescence intensity (at 625 nm) of CDs@S-DAQ probe with increase of GSH concentration (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15 and 20 mM). (C) Linear calibration plot of fluorescence intensity (at 625 nm) versus GSH concentrations (0.5–10 mM). F and F
0 represent fluorescence intensity at 625 nm in the presence and absence of GSH, respectively.

Cell viability of (A) HBMECs and (B) U87 cells treated with different concentrations of CDs@S-DAQ probe (incubating for 24 h). (C) Average intracellular fluorescence intensity of U87 cells incubated with CDs@S-DAQ probe at different time points. Fluorescence images of (D) HBMECs, (E) HBMECs + GSH, (F) U87 cells, (G) U87 cells + GSH and (H) U87 cells + NEM incubated with CDs@S-DAQ probe for 9 h.

(A) Schematic illustration of CDs@S-DAQ probe synthesis and sensing mechanism of CDs@S-DAQ probe to GSH. (B) Schematic illustration of fluorescence imaging of HBMECs and U87 cells using CDs@S-DAQ probe.

head.ce:title: Thioether-bridged carbon dots-dopamine quinone conjugate probe for glutathione sensing and discrimination between glioblastoma and normal cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and specific recognition and imaging of glioblastoma (GBM) cells are crucial for early diagnosis in GBM. Herein, a novel thioether-bridged fluorescence carbon dots (CDs@S for short)-dopamine quinone (DAQ) conjugate (termed as CDs@S-DAQ) probe with red emission was fabricated for sensitive sensing of glutathione (GSH) and selective fluorescence imaging of GBM cells. Due to the strong photo-induced electron transfer (PET) effect between DAQ (the acceptor) and CDs@S (the donor), the red fluorescence of CDs@S is significantly quenched. In the presence of GSH, on the one hand, GSH reduces the quinone structure to a hydroquinone structure via Michael addition reaction, which eliminates the PET effect between DAQ and CDs@S, resulting in the fluorescence restoring of CDs@S; on the other hand, it simultaneously induces the cleavage of the thioether bond linking DAQ and CDs, causing DAQ to detach from the CDs surface and reducing the PET effect, which leads to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. It has been demonstrated that the response of CDs@S-DAQ probe to GSH has a good linear relationship in the range from 0.5 to 10 mM, with an R2 value of 0.9978. Meanwhile, the as-fabricated CDs@S-DAQ probe has excellent stability, superior optical properties and low cytotoxicity. Based on these findings, the probe can be applied in live-cell fluorescence imaging, enabling discrimination between GBM cells and normal tissue cells according to their distinct GSH expression levels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glioblastoma (GBM) is one of the most aggressive and deadly malignant tumors of the central nervous system. Due to the lack of effective or sufficient treatment options, the one-year survival rate after diagnosis is only 25% [1–3]. Sensitive and specific recognition and imaging of GBM cells are critical for the early diagnosis of GBM, ultimately helping to improve the quality of life and survival time for patients. The rapid development of imaging technologies has enabled real-time, dynamic, in vivo and in vitro, qualitative and quantitative monitoring of various complex biological processes, such as gene expression, substance transport, signal transduction and protein interactions [4–6]. According to different principles, imaging techniques include computed tomography, positron emission tomography, single photon emission computed tomography, ultrasound imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, Raman imaging, photoacoustic imaging, fluorescence imaging, etc [7,8]. Among these techniques, fluorescence imaging has garnered considerable interest in recent years due to its high sensitivity, high spatial resolution, low cost, real-time data acquisition and the absence of ionizing radiation [9].

To date, several methods for fluorescence imaging of GBM cells have been developed. For example, Yang et al. introduced a water-soluble near-infrared (NIR) dye, heptamethine sulfoindocyanine IRDye78-functionalized probe (IRDye78-α-LADFO-89Zr), for multimodal imaging of GBM cells [10]; An et al. developed a dipolar two-photon excited fluorescent oxazepine dye-based probe for targeted optical imaging of GBM cells [11]; Liang et al. functionalized CdSeTe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) with folic acid to actively target glioma cells and achieve NIR fluorescence imaging [12]; Xue et al. employed CuInS2/ZnS QDs for fluorescence imaging of glioma cells [13]; and Peng et al. constructed a mouse glioma cell line stably expressing a large stokes-shifted yellow fluorescent protein for multicolor immunofluorescence imaging of the glioma microenvironment [14]. Although these studies have shown excellent performance in highly selective recognition and imaging of glioma cells, the inherent limitations of the fluorescent materials used have restricted their practical application. For instance, traditional organic fluorescent dyes exhibit poor optical stability, photobleaching and spectral overlap; conventional QDs (e.g., II-VI and III-V semiconductor QDs) pose cytotoxicity concerns; and fluorescent proteins is time-consuming and costly [15–18]. Therefore, the development of novel fluorescent nanomaterials for reliable and accurate long-term imaging of GBM cells is of great significance.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with a carbon core structure and a size of less than 10 nm [19,20]. CDs possess unique properties such as high fluorescence quantum yield, good optical stability, easy surface functionalization, excellent water solubility, low cytotoxicity, superior biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence and low cost, making them promising tools for live-cell fluorescence imaging [6,21–23]. However, most of the reported CDs require short-wavelength excitation (e.g., ultraviolet light) and emit blue or blue-green fluorescence (λem = 400–520 nm). The short-wavelength excitation light causes photodamage to biological tissues and exhibits limited tissue penetration depth. Additionally, the irradiation of short-wavelength light induces strong blue autofluorescence in biological tissues, resulting in significant background interference that hinders the extraction and analysis of the fluorescence signals for CDs [24,25]. These limitations severely restrict their application in biological imaging. In contrast, red-emitting CDs (λem > 600 nm) offer advantages such as deeper tissue penetration, reduced photodamage to biological samples and negligible autofluorescence background in complex biological systems [6,25–28]. As a result, red-emitting CDs have gained increasing attention for fluorescence imaging of tumor cells.

Due to uncontrolled cell growth, abnormal gene expression and metabolic imbalance, several physiological parameters in tumor cells differ significantly from those in normal tissue cells, such as glutathione (GSH) [29,30]. GSH is a tripeptide composed of glutamic acid, cysteine and glycine linked by peptide bonds. As the most abundant thiol in cells, it plays essential roles in immune regulation, antioxidant defense, cellular protection and detoxification [31–33]. Notably, due to increased oxidative stress within the cellular environment, GSH accumulates excessively in tumor cells, with concentrations (1–10 mM) significantly higher than those in normal tissue cells (1–2 mM) [34]. This concentration difference provides an opportunity to distinguish tumor cells from normal cells and holds great potential for early and accurate diagnosis of tumor.

Taking the above into consideration, a novel thioether (S)-bridged fluorescence CDs-dopamine quinone (DAQ) conjugate (abbreviated as CDs@S-DAQ) probe with red emission was fabricated. The CDs@S-DAQ probe has excellent stability, superior optical properties, low cytotoxicity and differential response to various concentrations of GSH, which can sensitively recognize GSH and selectively discriminate between human brain microvascular endothelial cells (HBMECs) and human GBM cells (U87 cells) by fluorescence method. This offers a promising tool for early diagnosis of GBM.

Citric acid, urea, 1-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), DA hydrochloride, GSH, 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8), thiodiglycolic anhydride and N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), formamide, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dichlormethane, sodium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide, hydrochloric acid and acetone were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout for all the experiments. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. The HBMECs and U87 cells were purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (Manassas, USA). HBMECs and U87 cells were cultured in DMEM medium containing fetal bovine serum (10%, v/v) at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator.

Fluorescence spectra were determined by a Hitachi F-7000 spectrofluorometer with a xenon lamp and a quartz cuvette (optical length 10 mm) (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained from a JEM-1400Flash instrument (JEOL, Japan). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image was taken on a TALOS F200 instrument (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a TENSOR 27 spectrometer with a spectral range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 and a resolution of 2 cm−1 (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The CCK-8 assay was measured with an Infinite 200 pro microplate reader (Tecan, Switzerland). The cellular fluorescence imaging studies was carried out using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Leica, German).

In a typical synthesis, citric acid (1.0 g) and urea (2.0 g) were first dissolved in DMF (10 mL) and then transferred to a 15 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The mixture was heated at 180°C for 8 h. After cooling, it was mixed with NaOH solution (50 mg/mL, 20 mL) for 10 min. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 16000 rpm for 15 min and dried under vacuum. The powder was finally dissolved in HCl solution (5%, v/v, 20 mL), stirred for 10 min, and centrifuged again at 16000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitate was washed three times with acetone and deionized water, respectively. After further dried in vacuum, CDs was obtained.

Varying the citric acid amount (6.0 g, 4.0 g, 2.0 g and 0.66 g), hydrothermal reaction temperature (160°C and 200°C), or hydrothermal reaction time (4 h, 6 h, 10 h and 12 h), different batches of CDs were synthesized.

Initially, CDs (10 mg) were dispersed in DMF (40 mL) by sonication for 1 h and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Subsequently, thiodiglycolic anhydride (100 mg) and DMAP (100 mg) were added under a nitrogen atmosphere at 0°C, and the mixture was stirred magnetically at room temperature for 24 h. Thereafter, deionized water (40 mL) was introduced to quench the reaction. The solution was then extracted three times using dichloromethane (40 mL), and the aqueous phase was collected. The aqueous phase was further concentrated in a rotary evaporator at 50°C, and the concentrate was mixed with an excess of acetone, stirred for 5 min, and left at −20°C for 30 min. The precipitate was collected through centrifuging for 15 min at 10000 rpm, and then washed three times with acetone and deionized water, respectively. After further dried in vacuum, CDs@S was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@S-DA and CDs@S-DAQ conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize CDs@S-DA conjugate, CDs@S (5 mg), EDC (5 mg) and NHS (5 mg) was dissolved in DMF (20 mL), the mixture was stirred at 0°C for 30 min, and then DA hydrochloride (50 mg) was added. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 16 h at 25°C under a nitrogen atmosphere. The product was further dialyzed against deionized water in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 36 h to remove excessive DA, EDC, NHS and possibly generated polydopamine. After centrifuging the solution (inside the dialysis bag) at 10000 rpm for 10 min, the precipitate was collected to obtain CDs@S-DA conjugate.

The CDs@S-DA was redissolved in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) and left to naturally oxidize in a refrigerator at 4°C for one week. The resulting product was vacuum-dried to yield CDs@S-DAQ conjugate as probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of GSH in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, different concentrations of GSH were first mixed with CDs@S-DAQ probe (40 μg/mL) dissolved in PBS (50 mM, pH 6.0). The mixtures were then incubated with gentle agitation for 1 h at 37°C. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected with excitation at 550 nm, and the fluorescence intensities at 625 nm were recorded for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity assay of CDs@S-DAQ probe
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CDs@S-DAQ probe on HBMECs and U87 cells were assessed using the CCK-8 assay. In brief, HBMECs and U87 cells were first seeded in a 96-well plate (5 × 103 cells per well) and allowed to adhere overnight, respectively. The cells were then treated with varying concentrations of CDs@S-DAQ probe (0–200 μg/mL) for 24 h at 37°C. Following this, add CCK-8 (10%, 100 μL) to each well and continue incubation for another 2 h. Finally, the absorbance of each well was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader to determine the cytotoxicity.

The cell viability rate was calculated according to equation (1):
(1)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
r a t e =
(
A X
−
A 0
A C
−
A 0
)
× 100 %
where
A X
is the absorbance of the experimental group (i.e., the cells were treated by CDs@S-DAQ probe),
A C
is the absorbance of the control group (i.e., the cells were untreated by CDs@S-DAQ) and
A 0
is the absorbance of the blank group (i.e., the cells were untreated by CCK-8).

In short, U87 cells were first plated in glass dishes at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and incubated overnight for cell attachment. CDs@S-DAQ probe (40 μg/mL) was then incubated with the U87 cells for diverse times (0–24 h). After incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the free CDs@S-DAQ probe. Finally, the cellular uptake was visualized using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

The fluorescence recovery of CDs@S-DAQ probe induced by GSH was studied in HBMECs and U87 cells, respectively. HBMECs and U87 cells were seeded in glass dishes at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and incubated overnight to allow cell attachment. After different pre-treatments, the cells were incubated with CDs@S-DAQ probe (40 μg/mL) for 9 h. The treatment conditions were as follows: (1) HBMECs without pre-treatment, (2) U87 cells without pre-treatment, (3) HBMECs pretreated with GSH (1 mM) at 37°C for 30 min, (4) U87 cells pretreated with GSH (1 mM) at 37°C for 30 min, and (5) U87 cells pretreated with NEM (1 mM) at 37°C for 30 min.
After incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove free GSH, NEM and/or residual CDs@S-DAQ probe, and images were captured using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of CDs@S-DAQ probe
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 A illustrates the design strategy for CDs@S-DAQ probe, which is used for the selective discrimination between HBMECs and U87 cells (Scheme 1B). The probe preparation‌ includes four-step processes: (1) one-step hydrothermal synthesis of CDs with emission wavelength of 625 nm using citric acid, urea and DMF as the carbon source, nitrogen source and reaction solvent, respectively; (2) bridging of thioether bond on the surface of CDs by reacting the hydroxyl and amino groups of CDs with thiodiglycolic anhydride under catalysis of DMAP to form CDs@S, (3) covalent attachment of dopamine (DA) to the surface of CDs@S through the reaction between carboxyl groups of CDs@S and amino group of DA to prepare CDs@S-DA, and (4) natural oxidation of DA (from CDs@S-DA) in air to DAQ to obtain CDs@S-DAQ probe.

To construct high-quality red-emitting CDs, three key preparation conditions were optimized: (a) the mass ratio of citric acid to urea, (b) the hydrothermal reaction temperature and (c) the hydrothermal reaction time. The optimized conditions are as follows: (a) 1:2, (b) 180°C and (c) 8 h (Fig. S1). Additionally, the reaction times for the surface modification of CDs with thioether bonds and for the subsequent modification of CDs@S with DA were optimized, yielding 24 h (Fig. S2A) and 16 h (Fig. S2B and 2C), respectively. Detailed characterization data are provided in the Supplementary Material.

After preparing CDs@S-DAQ probe under optimal conditions mentioned above, the morphology, size, surface functional groups and elemental composition were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), FT-IR spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. Fig. 1 A displays the TEM image of CDs@S-DAQ probe, revealing that the probe has relatively uniform sphere shapes with good monodispersity and no significant aggregation. The HRTEM image shows clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon, indicating that the probe has good crystallinity (Fig. 1B). Additionally, the size of probe was measured and analyzed by Nano Measurer software, which demonstrate a normal distribution of particle sizes with an average diameter of approximately 3.67 nm and a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.261 (Fig. 1C). Notably, the vascular walls of normal tissues only allow small molecules (<1 nm) to pass through. In contrast, the rapid growth of tumor tissue causes loose endothelial cell arrangement, weak pericyte attachment and an incomplete basement membrane, which enables the tumor vasculature to permit the passage of larger nanoparticles [35,36]. Therefore, the size of CDs@S-DAQ probe provides an inherent advantage for selective entry into tumor tissue cells.

FTIR spectra of CDs, CDs@S, CDs@S-DA and CDs@S-DAQ are presented in Fig. 1D. CDs exhibits characteristic absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1, 3147 cm−1, 2950 cm−1, 1645 cm−1, 1570 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1 (line (ⅰ) in Fig. 1D), which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–OH, N–H, C–H, CO/CN, CC and C–N, respectively. These peaks suggest that the surface of CDs possess numerous oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups, which are responsible for high water dispersibility. Compared to FT-IR spectrum of CDs, that of CDs@S shows new peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1210 cm−1 (line (ⅱ) in Fig. 1D). The former suggests the formation of new ester/amide bonds due to the reaction between thiodiglycolic anhydride and the hydroxyl/amino groups on the surface of CDs, and the latter is assigned to the stretching vibration of C–S bonds, confirming the successful bridging of thioether bonds on CDs surface. Furthermore, compared to FT-IR spectrum of CDs@S-DA (line (ⅲ) in Fig. 1D), the absorption peak of CDs@S-DAQ at 3420 cm−1 is reduced (line (ⅳ) in Fig. 1D), indicating the oxidation of DA to form a quinone structure.

In Fig. 1E, The XPS spectrum of CDs reveals three main peaks at 285.2 eV, 399.8 eV and 531.2 eV, corresponding to C, N and O elements, with atomic ratios of 60.75%, 18.36% and 20.89%, respectively. Fig. 1F shows that CDs@S contain not only the same C, N and O elements as CDs but also S (163.0 eV) element, with an atomic percentage of 4.48%, implying the presence of thioether bonds. After the surface modification of CDs@S with DA and subsequent natural oxidation to form CDs@S-DAQ, the XPS spectrum (Fig. 1G) shows a decrease in the S element peak and an increase in the peaks for C, N and O.

The optical properties of CDs@S-DAQ probe were studied in detail by a series of spectroscopy analysis. In Fig. S3, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs exhibits an obvious peak at 535 nm in the visible range, attributed to the graphite nitrogen in the carbon sp2 lattice. This nitrogen center can inject excess electrons into the unoccupied π∗ orbitals, significantly reducing the HOMO-LUMO energy gap and the corresponding optical transition energy [37,38]. Additionally, the oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl) on the CD surface can narrow the energy levels, leading to long-wavelength absorption [39]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@S features a new peak at 280 nm compared to CDs, which can be ascribed to the n→π∗ transitions of CO of ester/amide bond [40]. The absorption peak of CDs@S-DAQ at 280 nm decreases due to the oxidation of DA to form DAQ [41].

Fig. 1H shows that CDs, CDs@S and CDs@S-DAQ all emit fluorescence at 625 nm under optimal excitation at 550 nm. Compared to CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@S slightly decreases, while that of CDs@S-DAQ significantly decreases by 72.8%. The significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of CDs@S-DAQ is attributed to the fact that DA (a catechol structure) is sensitive to oxidative environments and readily oxidizes to DAQ (a quinone structure) in air. DAQ, as a typical electron acceptor, can prominently quench the fluorescence of the electron donor CDs through photo-induced electron transfer (PET) effect (Scheme 1A) [42].

Stability is crucial for the practical use of fluorescent probes in complex biological matrices. Therefore, the optical stability of CDs and CDs@S was investigated and compared. Fig. 2 A presents that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased by 54.52%, whereas that of CDs@S only decreased by 31.71%, after continuous irradiation under a UV light (365 nm) for 12 h. This suggests that CDs@S possess enhanced resistance to photobleaching. Fluorescence intensities of CDs and CDs@S dispersed in PBS at varying pH levels were measured, with results shown in Fig. 2B. Under strongly acidic conditions (pH 2–4), both CDs and CDs@S exhibited weak fluorescence. Within the pH range of 5–8, they displayed high fluorescence intensity with no significant changes as pH varied, suggesting stability under normal physiological pH and in the weakly acidic environment of tumor cells. Above pH 8, the fluorescence of both CDs and CDs@S increased significantly, likely due to the deprotonation of the carboxyl groups on their surfaces under alkaline conditions, leading to particle repulsion and a reduction of aggregation-induced quenching. Overall, CDs@S demonstrated superior pH stability compared to CDs. Additionally, the time-dependent stability of CDs and CDs@S at different pH levels was examined, as shown in Fig. 2C and D. Under strongly alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity of both increased initially and then decreased over time. Between pH 2 and pH 10, their fluorescence intensities remained essentially unchanged over 32 h. These results confirm the excellent optical stability of CDs@S, enabling CDs@S-based nanomaterials to maintain stable fluorescence in practical applications and offering a significant advantage in long-term cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: In the microenvironment of GBM cells, GSH levels are significantly higher than those in normal human tissue cells, and thus monitoring the levels of GSH to differentiate GBM cells from normal tissue cells has been of great interest [43]. Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs@S-DAQ exhibits a sensitive fluorescence response in the presence of GSH, switching from an “off” to an “on” state. Thus, CDs@S-DAQ can serve as a fluorescent probe for the determination of GSH. To investigate the analytical performance of CDs@S-DAQ probe, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the probe and various concentrations of GSH from 0.5 to 20 mM under 550 nm excitation was examined. As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, the fluorescence intensities at 625 nm of the probe gradually increase with increment of GSH concentration, but tend to stabilize when the GSH concentration exceeds 10 mM. This phenomenon may arise from all DAQ molecules on the surface of probe are almost reduced and all thioether bonds on the surface of probe are almost cleaved, resulting in maximal fluorescence recovery. An ideal linear relationship for GSH in the range of 0.5–10 mM is established, with the linear regression equation being F - F 0 = 240 C GSH + 516.1 and R2 = 0.9978 (Fig. 3C). According to the 3σ/S rule, the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 69 μM. Notably, the significant difference in fluorescence intensity of the probe in response to high and low concentrations of GSH suggests its potential to distinguish between tumor cells and normal cells.

The CDs@S-DAQ probe achieves fluorescence recovery in the presence of GSH through two primary mechanisms (Scheme 1A). On the one hand, DAQ undergoes a Michael addition reaction with GSH to form a thiol-DA conjugate, which disrupts electron transfer from CDs to DAQ, thereby restoring the fluorescence of CDs [44,45]. On the other hand, the thioether bond, which is sensitive to reductive environments, gradually breaks down under the influence of GSH [46,47], which causes DAQ to detach from the surface of CDs and reduces the PET effect between them, leading to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. To validate the cleavage of the thioether bond and DAQ detachment, XPS characterization and fluorescence comparative experiments were implemented. First, XPS analysis of CDs (obtained after reacting CDs@S-DAQ with GSH) revealed a significant decrease in content of S element (from ∼0.86% to ∼0%, Fig. 1G and S4). Ulteriorly, a controlled experiment was conducted (Fig. S5). After separately treating CDs@S-DAQ (with thioether bonds) and CDs-DAQ (without thioether bonds) with GSH (10 mM), the former exhibited a fluorescence recovery efficiency of 293%, while the latter showed only 69.2%. Subsequent addition of H2O2 (oxidizing agent, 10 mM) to regenerate the quinone structure of DAQ resulted in negligible fluorescence change of CDs@S-DAQ (272%), whereas fluorescence of CDs-DAQ significantly decreased (12.9%) (Fig. S5B and S5C). These results demonstrate that thioether bond cleavage and DAQ detachment are one of the dominant pathways for fluorescence recovery of CDs@S-DAQ.

To assess the specificity of the CDs@S-DAQ probe for GSH detection, various potential interfering substances including metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Fe3+), anions (NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2− and SCN−), small organic molecules (glucose, alanine (Ala), aspartic acid (Asp), arginine (Arg), glutamate (Glu), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), valine (Val), histidine (His), phenylalanine (Phe) and cysteine (Cys)) and cellular lysates (NEM-pretreated HBMECs and U87 cells) were investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, most substances exhibit negligible interference with GSH detection, except for high concentrations of Ca2+ (500 μM), S2O3 2− (500 μM), Ala (1 mM), Gly (1 mM), Phe (1 mM) and Cys (5 mM). However, these tested concentrations significantly exceed typical intracellular levels, particularly for S2O3 2− that are virtually absent in cells. These high concentrations of interferents were selected solely to evaluate the stability of the CDs@S-DAQ probe under extreme conditions, rather than in practical application scenarios. Notably, the fluorescence recovery efficiency induced by 200 μM of Cys (not exceeding 200 μM in both tumor and normal cells [48]), the mixture of low-concentration interferents, HBMECs lysate and U87 cells lysate was 2.16%, 4.68%, 3.87% and 2.84%, respectively, indicating that these physiological concentration interferents will not interfere with the selective response to GSH. Meanwhile, the pronounced changes are observed by the introduction of GSH, which reflects the excellent specificity of CDs@S-DAQ to GSH.

The excellent stability, good optical properties and GSH-responsivity of CDs@S-DAQ probe prompted us to evaluate its fluorescence imaging performance for GSH in living cells. Initially, the cytotoxicity of CDs@S-DAQ probe towards HBMECs and U87 cells was assessed using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, after incubation with various concentrations of probe for 24 h, the viability of both HBMECs and U87 cells remained above 90%, even at a high concentration of 200 μg/mL. These results indicate that the probe possesses good biocompatibility and biosafety, making it suitable for imaging analysis of living cells.

Subsequently, cellular uptake experiments were conducted. In brief, U87 cells were cultured with CDs@S-DAQ probe (40 μg/mL) for various durations, and their fluorescence was imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope with excitation at 550 nm. Fig. S7 and 4C demonstrate that the cells exhibited strong red fluorescence after 3 h of incubation, with the intensity increasing over time, indicating effective uptake of probe by the cells. The fluorescence intensity peaked after a 9 h incubation and remained strong even after 24 h. Simultaneously, it was observed that the morphology of U87 cells stayed unchanged even with prolonged exposure to 40 μg/mL of CDs@S-DAQ probe (Fig. S7), confirming the outstanding biocompatibility of CDs@S-DAQ.

To evaluate the feasibility of CDs@S-DAQ probe for distinguishing between normal and tumor cells based on its GSH responsiveness, HBMECs and U87 cells were utilized as research models, and the fluorescence images and intensities of both cell types after 9 h incubation with CDs@S-DAQ probe were observed and compared (Fig. 4D–G and S8). Almost no fluorescence was detected in HBMECs after co-incubation with the probe (Fig. 4D), while prominent red fluorescence was observed in U87 cells (Fig. 4F). To verify the fluorescence-activating effect of GSH on CDs@S-DAQ probe, both HBMECs and U87 cells were pretreated with GSH (1 mM) prior to a 9 h incubation with probe, respectively. This pretreatment led to the enhanced red fluorescence in both cell types (Fig. 4E and G) compared to those without GSH pretreatment (Fig. 4D and F). Furthermore, U87 cells pretreated with NEM (a thiol scavenger) exhibited a significant reduction in red fluorescence after incubation with probe for 9 h (Fig. 4H). These findings suggest that CDs@S-DAQ probe can respond to the high GSH expression in GBM cells with a significant fluorescence recovery, while maintaining fluorescence “off” in normal HBMECs with low GSH expression, making it suitable for the visualization of intracellular GSH and selective imaging of tumor cells.

Furthermore, the fluorescence sensing and imaging performance of CDs@S-DAQ probe are also compared with those of previous works, and the results are listed in Table S1 [22,49–57]. The linear range (0.5–10 mM) for GSH detection using the CDs@S-DAQ probe closely matches the GSH concentrations in the tumor microenvironment (1–10 mM) [34], which is wider than those of previous probes [22,54–57]. Although LOD (69 μM) of GSH using CDs@S-DAQ probe is slightly higher than those of previous probes [22,51,54–57], its red emission (625 nm) significantly reduces background interference from tissue autofluorescence and enhances penetration depth, surpassing probes with blue/green emission [22,50–54,56]. Moreover, CDs@S-DAQ achieve fluorescence “on” mode through a dual-response mechanism (GSH-induced reduction of the quinone and cleavage of the thioether bond), improving sensitivity and specificity, which is more favorable for live-cell imaging compared to “off” mode fluorescence probes [22,54]. As a CDs-based probe, CDs@S-DAQ also exhibits low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility and good stability, surpassing metal nanoclusters (NCs), organic dye or QDs [49,51–54]. Crucially, the CDs@S-DAQ probe successfully distinguishes U87 cells from HBMECs, highlighting its potential for early tumor diagnosis.

In summary, a novel “off-on” fluorescence probe based on CDs@S-DAQ conjugate has been developed for sensitive sensing of GSH and selective fluorescence imaging of GBM cells. The key factors contributing to the success of this work are as follows: (1) CDs@S exhibit red emission (λem = 625 nm), which offers advantages over short-wavelength fluorescence materials, including deeper tissue penetration, lower light-induced damage to biological samples and minimal background interference from autofluorescence in complex biological systems, making them suitable for biological analysis; (2) two GSH-induced fluorescence recovery pathways of CDs@S-DAQ probe, including the reduction of DAQ to DA and the cleavage of thioether bonds, result in outstanding responsiveness of the probe to GSH; (3) the CDs@S-DAQ probe possesses excellent stability, good optical properties and low cytotoxicity, making it a powerful tool for long-term and specific imaging of tumor cells. Importantly, the new CDs@S-DAQ probe can make a distinction between GBM cells (U87 cells) and normal tissue cells (HBMECs) by fluorescence imaging based on the different levels of GSH expression in the two types of cells. The current work offers a promising approach for the early diagnosis of tumors. However, the CDs@S-DAQ probe is only limited to GSH sensing and discrimination between tumor and normal cells. In the future, we will further optimize the probe's preparation process, quantify the cellular uptake efficiency of probe in tumor and normal cells and evaluate the probe's BBB penetrability for clinical applications.

Ningcheng Wei: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yi Xiao: Validation, Software, Data curation. Yang Jin: Software, Resources. Liying Zhang: Software, Resources. Panpan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Shuhu Du: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
